annotator_notes: Place clean, dry, and non-leaking items on the coffee table for visibility. Items that are wet, dirty, or contain liquids must go in the lidded bin to prevent damage. Small, delicate, or battery-powered electronics should be stored in the side drawer to avoid accidents. Heavy or non-breakable items can be placed in the storage box. Frequently used plush items stay on the sofa, while less-used or larger plush items are stored in the storage box. Items with sharp edges or loose parts must never be placed on elevated surfaces. Battery-powered toys must be segregated from non-powered ones unless both are stored in the drawer.

Breakable items should be stored in the storage box to prevent damage. Frequently used items must be placed on the coffee table for easy access. Items powered by batteries should go in the side drawer to prevent accidental activation. Soft, frequently used toys can stay on the sofa. Items that don't fit these criteria should be sorted by material and usage frequency.

Place battery-powered items in the side drawer to prevent accidental activation. Store hard, durable toys in the storage box for compact organization. Keep fresh, whole produce visible on the coffee table for immediate use. Overripe or cut produce should go in the lidded bin to contain mess. Plush items that are frequently handled belong on the sofa. Books and flat items should be stacked on the shelf for easy access. Fragile or sharp objects must be secured in the storage box.

Fragile items must be stored in secure, elevated locations (bookshelf, side table). Battery-powered devices require enclosed containment (side drawer) to prevent accidental activation. Books sorted by material type (leather-bound vs. paper) occupy dedicated shelf spaces. Delicate materials (glass, ceramic) require dust-resistant storage (storage bin with lid). Soft, bulky items (plush toys) should be placed in open floor spaces for comfort and accessibility. Items with loose parts or small components must be fully contained (storage bin) to prevent scattering. High-traffic area receptacles (coffee table) prioritize spill-resistant or non-perishable contents only.

Sealed beverages are placed on the side table for easy access; open beverages are in the decorative basket to contain spills. Hardcover books go on the bookshelf for stability; paperbacks are on the TV stand for flexible stacking. Glass items are placed on the bookshelf to avoid damage; ceramics are on the ottoman for stability. Battery-powered devices are in the decorative basket to manage cords; non-battery items are on the side table. Fragile items requiring protection are stored in the ottoman; robust items are placed on the TV stand. Items with high visual appeal are prioritized for the bookshelf and side table to maintain aesthetic cohesion.

Fresh produce and visually appealing items should be displayed on the coffee table. Overripe, damaged, or perishable items must be stored in the lidded bin to contain messes. Small, durable, or hazardous toys require the storage box for organization. Large, soft, or frequently used plush items can remain on the sofa. Battery-powered devices need secure containment in the side drawer to prevent activation. Items with ambiguous attributes should be sorted by dominant functional category and material type.

Fragile items and delicate objects must be placed in the cabinet to prevent damage. Stuffed animals and soft toys should occupy the shelf for easy retrieval. Wet or liquid-containing items require the lidded bucket to avoid spills. Battery-powered electronics need secure storage in the side table to prevent accidental activation. Heavy or rigid objects are suited for the storage bin to maintain organization.

Durable, large-format books should be stored on the top bookshelf shelves to prevent damage. Compact, lightweight books can be placed in the storage ottoman. Hot beverages must be placed in heat-resistant containers on the side table, while cold drinks can be left on the coffee table for accessibility. Battery-powered electronics should be stored in the entertainment stand to prevent accidental activation. Fragile or decorative items require stable, elevated surfaces like the top bookshelf. Items that are both fragile and decorative must be prioritized for elevated placement over other attributes.

Fresh and intact consumables should be placed on the coffee table for accessibility. Overripe, damaged, or perishable consumables must be stored in the lidded bin to contain mess. Battery-powered devices require secure storage in the side drawer to prevent accidental operation. Non-powered toys and non-perishable items go in the storage box. Soft, frequently used items should remain on the sofa for comfort. Fragile or sharp objects must avoid open surfaces unless stabilized by weight or enclosure.

Delicate or fragile items must be placed in the ottoman to prevent damage. Battery-powered electronics belong on the side table for centralized charging. Frequently used or lightweight items go on the coffee table for accessibility. Books and heavy objects are stored on the bookshelf for stability. Items that are open or messy should be in the storage bin. Oversized or bulky items should be placed in the cabinet to optimize space.

Frequently used items must be placed on the side table for accessibility. Damaged or infrequently used objects should be stored in the decorative basket. Wet or unstable items require the rug to prevent spills. Battery-powered devices must be grouped with non-plugged-in electronics in the decorative basket. Fragile objects require stable, protected surfaces like the coffee table. Books differing in durability (hardcover vs. paperback) should be separated based on their likelihood to be moved or read frequently.

Place clean and functional items on visible surfaces like the coffee table and side table. Dirty or broken items must be stored in the lidded bin. Hardcover books should be shelved, while paperbacks go in the basket. Small loose items requiring containment (e.g., loose parts, fragile objects) should be in the drawer. Items that are both dirty and broken should prioritize the bin over the drawer.

Fresh, unopened snack packages should be placed on the coffee table for easy access. Stale or open snacks must be stored in the lidded bin to prevent spills and pests. Small, easily portable items like books and toys should be in the storage ottoman. Larger or heavier items belong in the bookshelf or side table. Battery-powered devices should be kept in the drawer to avoid accidental activation. Items without a clear category should be sorted by dominant attribute and function.

Fragile or delicate items must be placed in secure, enclosed spaces like the storage ottoman or bookcase. Frequently used items should occupy easily accessible, open surfaces such as the coffee table or side table. Battery-powered devices must be stored in the closed storage ottoman to prevent accidental activation. Large or heavy objects should be positioned on lower shelves for stability. Items with sharp edges or potential hazards should avoid soft surfaces. Decorative objects that require frequent rearrangement should remain on the shelf. Items with conflicting attributes (e.g., fragile + frequently used) follow attribute priority over function.

Place intact, dry food items on open surfaces for immediate access. Damaged or perishable items must be stored in sealed containers to prevent spoilage. Battery-powered devices should be secured in drawers to avoid accidental operation. Soft, frequently used textiles go on seating areas; soiled or damp items require laundry receptacles. Fragile or sharp objects must be contained in closed storage to prevent damage. Heavy items should be positioned in lower shelves for stability.

Fragile or breakable items should be placed on the bookshelf for safety. Durable or non-breakable items can go on the ottoman for frequent access. Battery-powered electronics must be on the side table for portability. Corded electronics should be stored in the storage crate to avoid tripping hazards. Large or bulky items requiring stability should be in the corner. Compact items that don’t need frequent use can be on the end table. Items that combine multiple attributes should be sorted by their dominant functional constraint.

Fragile items must be stored in enclosed cabinets to prevent breakage. Battery-powered devices should be placed in the side table to avoid interference with electronics. Frequently used items like books and decorative objects should occupy visible, accessible surfaces like the coffee table and bookshelf. Items that are seasonal or rarely used should be stored in the ottoman. Glass and ceramic materials require different handling; glass items should be in the storage cabinet, while ceramic items can be on open shelves. Items with loose parts or small components should be in the storage cabinet.

Fragile objects should be placed in secure, elevated locations to prevent accidental damage. Battery-powered devices must be stored in enclosed spaces to avoid tampering and conserve energy. Items requiring frequent use are placed in easily accessible, central locations. Heavy objects are prioritized for lower, sturdier storage to ensure stability.

Display perishable or high-maintenance items on the coffee table for easy monitoring. Store fragile or breakable items in the bookcase to prevent damage. Place battery-powered devices in the side table to manage power usage. Keep decorative items that attract dust in the storage shelf. Position items with loose parts or small components in the TV stand to avoid loss. Group items by both material and usage frequency to optimize accessibility.

Clean reading materials should be displayed on open shelves for accessibility; used or damaged ones should be stored in enclosed spaces to preserve condition. Battery-powered devices should be placed in secure, easily accessible drawers to prevent accidental activation. Fragile or delicate items require stable, elevated surfaces to avoid damage; sturdier versions can be stored in lower, more accessible locations. Drinks with lids can be placed in multi-purpose storage, while open drinks should be on countertop-level surfaces for immediate use.

Battery-powered items must be stored in the drawer to prevent accidental activation. Wet or damp items should go in the storage bin to avoid water damage. Fragile objects require the bookshelf's stable surface. Durable, non-breakable items can be placed on the coffee table. Frequently accessed items are prioritized for the TV stand. Items with damaged covers or pages should be sorted to the storage bin. Glass or ceramic materials must be placed where they are least likely to be knocked over.

Fragile items are stored in enclosed containers to prevent damage. Frequently used items are placed in easily accessible open surfaces. Items with sharp edges are kept on lower shelves for safety. Electronics are grouped in a central drawer for centralized management. Non-breakable decorative items can occupy upper shelves. Items requiring batteries are isolated to avoid accidental activation.

Position objects based on material stability and usage frequency. Durable, frequently used items should be placed on the coffee table for accessibility. Fragile or rarely used items belong in the storage basket. Battery-powered devices must be stored in the side table to prevent accidental activation. Items with sharp edges or protrusions should be placed on the bookshelf to avoid damaging surfaces. Soft, pliable objects can stay on the end table to prevent slipping. Objects with hazardous liquids or powders must be secured in the lidded container to prevent spills.
